coredata.dc:title: Grain refining efficiency of Al–Ti–C alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Birol, Yücel
coredata.dc:description: The problems associated with boride agglomeration and the poisoning effect of Zr in Zr-bearing alloys have created a big demand for boron-free grain refiners. The potential benefits of TiC as a direct nucleant for aluminium grains have thus generated a great deal of interest in TiC-bearing alloys in recent years. In Al–Ti–C grain refiners commercially available today, Al3Ti particles are introduced into the melt along with the TiC particles. Since the latter are claimed to nucleate α-Al directly, it is of great technological interest to see if reducing the Ti:C ratio further, i.e., increasing the C content of the grain refiner, will produce an increase in the grain refining efficiency of these alloys. A series of grain refiner samples with the Ti concentration fixed at 3% and a range of C contents between 0 and 0.75 were obtained by appropriately mixing an experimental Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy with Al–10Ti alloy and commercial purity aluminium. The grain refining efficiency of these grain refiners was assessed to investigate the role of the insoluble TiC and the soluble Al3Ti particles. The optimum chemistry for the Al–Ti–C grain refiners was also identified.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metals
Casting
Al–Ti–C master alloy
Grain refinement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microstructure of the experimental Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy at two different magnifications.
The XRD spectrum of the experimental Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy.

The grain refining performance test results of the five grain refiner samples obtained by appropriately mixing the Al–3Ti–0.75C, Al–10Ti alloys and commercial purity aluminium; (a) grain refiner #1, (b) grain refiner #2, (c) grain refiner #3, (d) grain refiner #4 and (e) grain refiner #5.
Grain refiner samples, prepared by mixing Al–3Ti–0.75C, Al–10Ti alloys and commercial purity aluminium, and used in the inoculation of 3000g commercial purity aluminium

article.ce:title: Grain refining efficiency of Al–Ti–C alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Institute, Marmara Research Center, TÜBİTAK, 41470 Gebze, Kocaeli, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The problems associated with boride agglomeration and the poisoning effect of Zr in Zr-bearing alloys have created a big demand for boron-free grain refiners. The potential benefits of TiC as a direct nucleant for aluminium grains have thus generated a great deal of interest in TiC-bearing alloys in recent years. In Al–Ti–C grain refiners commercially available today, Al3Ti particles are introduced into the melt along with the TiC particles. Since the latter are claimed to nucleate α-Al directly, it is of great technological interest to see if reducing the Ti:C ratio further, i.e., increasing the C content of the grain refiner, will produce an increase in the grain refining efficiency of these alloys. A series of grain refiner samples with the Ti concentration fixed at 3% and a range of C contents between 0 and 0.75 were obtained by appropriately mixing an experimental Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy with Al–10Ti alloy and commercial purity aluminium. The grain refining efficiency of these grain refiners was assessed to investigate the role of the insoluble TiC and the soluble Al3Ti particles. The optimum chemistry for the Al–Ti–C grain refiners was also identified.

ce:section.ce:para: Grain refinement is widely used in aluminium foundries to promote a fine equiaxed grain structure with improved mechanical and cosmetic properties [1]. The vast majority of grain refining applications employ Al–Ti–B alloys which typically consist of the soluble Al3Ti and the insoluble TiB2 particles in an aluminium matrix with extremely small amounts of Ti and B in solution [2]. While a consensus has not been reached, the insoluble TiB2 particles are often credited for the grain-refining effect but excess Ti has also been claimed to play a key role [3–6]. The TiB2 particles in commercial Al–Ti–B master alloys, however, are rather coarse and have a tendency to agglomerate, leading to a number of quality problems. Streaking and porosity in thin foils, scratch-like linear surface defects in litho and bright-anodized sheet, internal cracking in extrusion billets, crack initiation in high strength aluminium alloy plate and forgings are often blamed on coarse TiB2 clusters [7,8].

These quality-related problems along with the filter blockage encountered in casting and the poisoning effect of Zr/Cr in Zr/Cr-bearing alloys are largely overcome with TiC-bearing grain refiners which were developed mainly in response to industry requirements for improved cleanliness and performance [9–18]. The cubic NaCl crystal structure of TiC is very similar to the fcc structure of α-Al rendering the nucleation of Al on TiC particles crystallographically favorable [19,20], suggesting that excess Ti may not be needed [21]. Experimental evidence for aluminium forming epitaxially on fine carbide particles of a commercial Al–Ti–C grain refiner was recently provided [22]. In Al–Ti–C grain refiners commercially available today, however, a large number of Al3Ti particles are introduced into the melt along with the TiC particles. Since the latter are credited for the grain refining effect [13,19,21], it is of great technological interest to see if increasing the C content and thus the population of the insoluble TiC particles will produce an increase in the grain refining efficiency of Al–Ti–C master alloys. A series of grain refiner samples with the Ti concentration fixed at 3% and a range of C contents between 0 and 0.75 were obtained by appropriately mixing an experimental Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy with Al–10Ti alloy and commercial purity aluminium. The grain refining efficiency of these grain refiners was assessed to investigate how grain refiners with only the insoluble TiC particles (i.e., no excess Ti) rates against those which provide extra Ti on top of the TiC stoichiometry. The optimum chemistry for the Al–Ti–C grain refiners was thus identified.

The grain refinement performance test involved melting of a commercial purity (99.7%) aluminium in a silicon carbide crucible using an electric resistance furnace. The temperature of the melt was maintained at 715±5°C throughout the test. Addition of the grain refiner sample was made so as to bring the Ti concentration of the melt to a level of 0.02% (Table 1). The melt was stirred with a graphite rod for 30s after inoculation. Samples were taken 2, 5, 10 and 15min after the addition using an alumina crucible which was held submerged in the melt for 30s before sampling. Each sample was cast into a cylindirical steel mold (Ø28mm×50mm) and then sectioned horizontally 20mm from the base. The sectioned surface was polished and macroetched in Poulton's reagent. The grain size in the inoculated samples was assessed pictorially.

The grain refinement test results for Al–3Ti, Al–3Ti–0.75C and three grain refiners with decreasing C and thus increasing excess Ti contents (Table 1) are illustrated in Fig. 4 . Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy, which introduces into the melt only the insoluble TiC particles, is apparently a poor grain refiner (Fig. 4e). Inoculation with the Al–3Ti alloy, with only the soluble Al3Ti particles, has also produced a coarse grain structure with columnar grains near the edges of the inoculated samples, similar to those obtained by Al–3Ti–0.75C (Fig. 4a). While the relatively poor performance of the binary Al–Ti alloy is not at all surprising, the latter, with a very high population of TiC particles, would be expected to perform better if TiC were, as claimed, a direct nucleant for aluminium [19–21].

The grain refinement of aluminium which is far from adequate when Al–3Ti–0.75C is used alone, is improved remarkably when excess Ti is made available through the use of the Al–10Ti master alloy in the mixture. One can see that each of the mixture grain refiners is superior than either of the parent alloys in terms of grain refining efficiency (Fig. 4b–d). A reduction in inoculated grain size is noted once the Ti:C ratio of the grain refiner is increased above the TiC stoichiometry (Fig. 4d) and the grain refinement becomes more prominent with increasing Ti:C ratios. The grain refiner sample with an average composition of Al–3Ti–0.15C, a common commercial alloy, appears to yield the smallest grain sizes (Fig. 4b). Complete conversion of coarse columnar grain structure to fine equiaxed grains occurred within 2min of inoculation with this particular grain refiner. The fine, equiaxed grain structure thus obtained was retained until 15min after inoculation. The holding time of the melt, employed in the present work, however, is too short to judge the stability of the carbide particles, which is reported to be limited in melts poor in Ti [24].

The processing and hence the microstructural features of the Al–Ti–C master alloys were shown to have a big impact on the grain refining efficiency of these alloys [25]. As the same Al–Ti and Al–Ti–C alloys, yet in different proportions, were used when preparing the mixtures, size and characteristics of TiC and Al3Ti particles and their agglomerates were essentially the same in each of these grain refiners. Hence, batch-to-batch variations in microstructural features, often encountered in similar master alloys, cannot be held responsible for the observed behavior.

Aluminium was shown to form epitaxially on bulk TiC [19] and on 10μm particles in a metal–matrix composite [20], and recently on the much finer carbide particles typical of a commercial Al–Ti–C grain refiner [22]. Nevertheless, increasing the C content and thus the population of the insoluble TiC particles does not produce an improvement in the grain refining efficiency of Al–Ti–C master alloys. The very high population of TiC particles which inevitably leads to clustering may, at least in part, be responsible for the poor performance of the Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy. The performance of the stoichiometric alloy is possibly impaired also by the increased rate of collision, agglomeration and settling of TiC particles, once introduced into the melt, thereby reducing the population of the viable nuclei further. The majority of the particles in the stoichiometric alloy were probably redundant and failed to contribute to the grain refining capacity yielding a very poor nucleation efficiency. Similar observations were made elsewhere [9].

While the cubic NaCl structure of TiC particles is more similar to the fcc structure of α-Al than the tetragonal TiB2 particles, the lattice mismatch between TiC and Al (4.328 and 4.048Å, respectively) is as much as 6.9%. Furthermore, TiC is reported to be a non-stoichiometric phase (Ti(1+x)C(1−x), 0≤ x ≤0.36) [26], whose lattice parameter varies with composition. Only a fraction of the carbide particles with a favorable epitaxial match with α-Al must have taken part in the nucleation process when the aluminium melt was inoculated with the Al–3Ti–0.75C alloy.

The results suggest that progressive additions of excess Ti into the melt along with the carbide particles clearly have a favorable effect on grain refinement which is maximized at a Ti:C ratio of around 20. The TiC particles are believed to facilitate direct nucleation of aluminium, while titanium in excess, contributes possibly as a powerful constitutional supercoolant, retarding grain growth. An extra Ti on top of the TiC stoichiometry ratio acts to slow the growth of the α-Al crystals, permitting greater undercooling to be reached, as it needs to partition between the solid and the liquid. This could explain why the Al–3Ti–0.15C alloy, which introduces into the melt a sufficient number of carbide particles along with the maximum amount of solute Ti, is a much more efficient grain refiner than the stoichiometric alloy and alloys with a lower Ti:C ratio.

Al–3Ti–0.75C does grain refine aluminium, yet rather poorly, possibly owing to a very high population of TiC particles which agglomerate both during production and after being released into the melt, leading to a poor nucleation efficiency. Besides, a relatively small fraction of the carbide particles with a favorable epitaxial match with α-Al must be taking part in the nucleation process. The grain refinement performance is improved notably when extra Ti on top of the TiC stoichiometry is made available in the melt. Excess Ti apparently plays a key role in the refinement of grain structure when using TiC-bearing grain refiners, by retarding grain growth. The Al–3Ti–0.15C grain refiner, a very popular commercial master alloy, provides the best performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: It is a pleasure to thank Mr. Osman Çakır of Marmara Research Center for his help in the experimental part of this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proceedings of the International Conference on Aluminum Alloys, JILM
Proceedings of the International Conference on Continous Casting
